name: Jayadratha
description: Jayadratha, the king of Sindhu, was a significant figure in the epic Mahabharata. He was the son of Vriddhakshatra and was granted a boon that he would be an invincible warrior for a single day. He married Dushala, the sister of the Kauravas, solidifying his alliance with them. Jayadratha is primarily known for his role in the abduction of Draupadi during the Pandavas' exile. While the Pandavas were away hunting, he attempted to force Draupadi to marry him. However, she resisted, and the Pandavas, upon their return, defeated him and subjected him to humiliation, shaving his head and leaving only five tufts of hair. This humiliation fueled his animosity towards the Pandavas, particularly Arjuna. During the Kurukshetra War, Jayadratha played a crucial role on the Kaurava side. He used his boon to prevent Arjuna from reaching Yudhishthira on the thirteenth day of the war, creating the circumstances that led to the death of Arjuna's son, Abhimanyu. This act enraged Arjuna, who vowed to kill Jayadratha before sunset the next day, or immolate himself. Through Krishna's divine intervention, Arjuna was able to fulfill his vow, leading to Jayadratha's death. His death was a pivotal moment in the war, symbolizing the triumph of dharma over adharma and highlighting the consequences of his actions. His story is a cautionary tale of pride, lust, and the misuse of power.
subtitle: King of Sindhu, Abductor of Draupadi

keyMyths.title: Abduction of Draupadi
keyMyths.description: During the Pandavas' exile, Jayadratha attempted to abduct Draupadi, but was thwarted and humiliated by the returning Pandavas. This incident created deep animosity between him and the Pandavas. It is described in detail in the Vana Parva of the Mahabharata.

keyMyths.title: Jayadratha's Boon
keyMyths.description: Jayadratha received a boon that he could not be defeated by anyone except Arjuna, and that too, only for one day. This boon allowed him to block Arjuna's path on the thirteenth day of the Kurukshetra War. This is a key element in the Drona Parva of the Mahabharata.

keyMyths.title: Death of Jayadratha
keyMyths.description: Arjuna vowed to kill Jayadratha before sunset after Abhimanyu's death. Krishna used his divine powers to create an illusion of sunset, prompting Jayadratha to reveal himself, allowing Arjuna to kill him. This is a dramatic moment described in the Drona Parva.

sources.author: John Dowson
sources.title: A Classical Dictionary of Hindu Mythology and Religion, Geography, History
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of characters and events in Hindu mythology, including Jayadratha's role in the Mahabharata.

sources.author: Krishna Dharma
sources.title: Mahabharata: The Greatest Spiritual Epic of All Time
sources.description: A modern retelling of the Mahabharata, offering insights into the motivations and significance of characters like Jayadratha.

associations.name: Dushala's Husband
associations.significance: Represents his alliance with the Kauravas and involvement in their conflicts.

associations.name: King of Sindhu
associations.significance: Highlights his royal status and the responsibilities associated with it.

associations.name: Invincibility for a Day
associations.significance: Illustrates the temporary nature of power and the consequences of its misuse.

associations.name: Abductor of Draupadi
associations.significance: Signifies his role as an antagonist to the Pandavas and a perpetrator of injustice.

associations.name: Son of Vriddhakshatra
associations.significance: Connects him to his ancestry and the legacy of his family.

associations.name: Kurukshetra War Participant
associations.significance: Indicates his involvement in the epic battle and his ultimate demise.

associations.name: Death by Arjuna
associations.significance: Symbolizes the karmic consequences of his actions and the triumph of dharma.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both characters are responsible for instigating major conflicts due to their actions involving women (Helen and Draupadi respectively).
Both characters are known for their deceitful actions and causing trouble for the main heroes.
Similar to Jayadratha, Set represents chaos and disruption, challenging the established order represented by other deities/heroes.

worshipPractices.name: Regional Deities
worshipPractices.description: While Jayadratha is not directly worshipped, regional deities in Sindh and surrounding areas, which were historically under his rule, may receive veneration that indirectly acknowledges his legacy. These practices often involve prayers for protection and prosperity, reflecting the roles and responsibilities associated with kingship.

cultural.modernLegacy: Jayadratha's story serves as a cautionary tale about the misuse of power and the consequences of adharma. His actions are often cited in discussions about ethics, morality, and the importance of righteous conduct, particularly in leadership roles. He is remembered as an antagonist whose actions contributed to the unfolding of the Kurukshetra War.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Lineage
extendedContent.content: Jayadratha was born to King Vriddhakshatra of the Sindhu kingdom. His father, desiring a son, had performed rigorous penances to obtain divine favor. It was prophesied that Jayadratha would be a powerful warrior, but also that he would be defeated by a reincarnation of Shiva. To counter this, Vriddhakshatra granted him a boon of invincibility for one day, hoping to circumvent the prophecy. Jayadratha's lineage connected him to the Kuru dynasty through his marriage to Dushala, the sister of the Kauravas. This alliance played a significant role in shaping his allegiances and actions during the events leading up to and including the Kurukshetra War. His early life was marked by the privileges and responsibilities of a royal heir, but also by the looming shadow of the prophecy and his father's attempts to avert it. This combination of factors shaped his character and ultimately influenced his destiny.

extendedContent.title: The Abduction of Draupadi
extendedContent.content: Jayadratha's most infamous act was his attempted abduction of Draupadi during the Pandavas' exile. While the Pandavas were away hunting, Jayadratha encountered Draupadi in the forest. Enamored by her beauty, he proposed marriage, which she vehemently refused. Angered by her rejection, Jayadratha forcibly tried to take her away with the help of his soldiers. The Pandavas, alerted to the situation, quickly returned and rescued Draupadi, defeating Jayadratha and his army. As punishment, Bhima shaved Jayadratha's head, leaving five tufts, and forced him to acknowledge the Pandavas as his masters. This humiliating defeat fueled Jayadratha's resentment and desire for revenge, further solidifying his alliance with the Kauravas and setting the stage for his role in the Kurukshetra War. The incident highlights his arrogance, lust, and disregard for dharma, contributing to his negative portrayal in the epic.

extendedContent.title: Death in the Kurukshetra War
extendedContent.content: During the Kurukshetra War, Jayadratha fought on the side of the Kauravas. On the thirteenth day of the war, he played a crucial role in preventing Arjuna from reaching Yudhishthira, thus trapping Abhimanyu in the Chakravyuha formation. Abhimanyu bravely fought but was ultimately killed by a group of Kaurava warriors, including Jayadratha. Arjuna, upon learning of Abhimanyu's death and Jayadratha's role in it, vowed to kill Jayadratha before sunset the next day, or immolate himself. The Kauravas hid Jayadratha, hoping to protect him and force Arjuna to commit suicide. However, Krishna orchestrated events to make it appear as if the sun had already set, prompting Jayadratha to reveal himself. At that moment, Arjuna, guided by Krishna, shot an arrow that severed Jayadratha's head, sending it flying into his father's lap, as per a boon granted to Vriddhakshatra. This fulfilled both the prophecy of Jayadratha's death and Arjuna's vow, marking the end of Jayadratha's life and his role in the epic.

shortDescription: Jayadratha, king of Sindhu, allied with the Kauravas and abducted Draupadi. His actions led to his death at Arjuna's hands during the Kurukshetra War.
